testingFrequency: Upon installation and annually thereafter for certain testable assemblies
dueBasis: Certain backflow prevention assemblies with test ports must be tested upon installation and thereafter annually by a licensed backflow tester. Frisco administers annual inspection fees through BSI.
coveredPropertyTypes: Properties with potential hazards identified by the Public Works Backflow Inspector
Irrigation systems with required backflow assemblies
Sites requiring plumbing permits and backflow approval before related inspections
officialListLabel: Use BSI to obtain the list of registered Frisco testers
penalties: Frisco charges a per-device annual inspection fee through the City in addition to private tester charges.
sourceExcerpt: Frisco says certain testable backflow assemblies must be tested upon installation and annually thereafter, and the City partners with BSI for program administration and registered tester access.
verdictSummary: Frisco requires installation testing and recurring annual testing for certain testable assemblies, with BSI handling much of the program workflow.
whoIsAffected: Properties where the hazard review requires a testable backflow assembly, including irrigation-related installations and other inspected hazards.
residentialNotes: Frisco homeowners run into this most often through irrigation systems and related permit work.
The City fee layer means even routine residential testing is not just a private contractor quote problem.
commercialNotes: Commercial sites should expect the hazard review to control whether a device is testable and annual.
Backflow approval can affect related inspection sequencing for larger projects.

annualTesting.summary: Frisco is not purely annual-for-everything. The City requires installation testing and then annual testing for certain testable assemblies identified by its program.
highlights: Certain testable assemblies are annual after installation.
The City layers a per-device annual inspection fee on top of tester pricing.
BSI is part of the core workflow, not an optional extra.
workflowSteps: Confirm that the assembly falls into Frisco's testable annual category.
Use a licensed tester and account for the City inspection fee.
Route reporting and tester lookup through the BSI-backed Frisco workflow.

irrigation.summary: Frisco irrigation work is one of the clearest local triggers because backflow approval can hold up related inspection flow.
highlights: Irrigation-related assemblies are part of the City's backflow review rather than a separate landscaping-only issue.
Missing backflow approval can disrupt irrigation inspection timing.
The City fee layer still applies where the assembly enters the annual program.
workflowSteps: Check whether the irrigation installation requires a testable backflow assembly.
Clear the backflow approval step before assuming irrigation inspections can close.
Keep the BSI and City fee workflow aligned with the install timeline.
Confirm with the Frisco program whether the property needs a testable backflow assembly.
Use a licensed tester and account for the City's per-device annual inspection fee.
Use BSI for tester discovery and program administration.
Keep installation permits and subsequent testing aligned with the City's inspection flow.
failureHighlights: Frisco separates tester cost from the City's annual inspection fee.
Irrigation inspection scheduling depends on backflow approval being complete.
Do not assume all assemblies are annual; the City's hazard review determines the requirement.

costBand.testingRange: Private tester pricing varies, plus a City-administered per-device annual inspection fee.
costBand.repairRetestRange: Repair and retest costs vary with assembly type and installation conditions.
costBand.pricingNotes: Frisco is one of the clearer cities on the existence of a City fee layered on top of private testing charges.
